title: The Citadel of Bureaucracy & Endless Destinies: The Clockwork City - a gamebook double feature!
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hrA_TspDTyM

Daniel (@dungeondive) runs a gamebook double feature, and both win him over by excelling at very different ambitions. The Citadel of Bureaucracy by JD Mitchell (a review copy) is a "Fighting Reality" parody of the Fighting Fantasy books: same skill/stamina/luck character sheet, but you play a beleaguered civil servant battling unreliable transit, IT, a Canada goose and creeping nihilism to clear your desk before vacation. It tracks time as a fail clock, and — comedy being subjective — Daniel was surprised how hard he laughed, helped by Matt Herring's old-school art wrapped around a mundane office setting (he keeps recognising Seattle landmarks).

The Clockwork City (Endless Destinies, by Karina Keefe, design by Joe Harris, art by Paula Zorite) attempts something newer: a gamebook powered by a 52-card deck. Each of four chapters has its own explorable map (Destiny Quest-style), and the cards drive a genuinely involved combat system — a buildable hero deck with advanced cards, enemy decks, a rising Peril track, and melee/ranged distance — while doubling as strength/skill/spirit skill checks. He finds the deeper, choice-rich combat a welcome change from rolling 2d6, and hopes it's the first of a series.

----

For gamebooks, do you lean toward sharp parody and writing like Citadel, or mechanical innovation like Clockwork City's card-driven combat?
